entity_library.Apoptosis text overlay: White text appearing in the lower right corner defining apoptosis as a 'Suicide program' (programmed cell death) of individual cells, e.g., by external signals, a process also mentioned by a male voice.
entity_library.Macrophage text overlay: White text appearing in the lower right corner defining macrophage as a phagocyte responsible for the removal of dead cells (cell debris) and more, a role also described by a male voice.
entity_library.B cell: A spiky, light-gray spherical cell that receives a 'starting signal for division and maturation' from an antigen, undergoing 'clonal expansion' to generate thousands of new cells, including plasmablasts and plasma cells, as described by the narrator.
entity_library.Plasma cell: A golden-yellow spherical cell with purple Y-shaped antibodies on its surface, shown rotating and interacting with small particles in a gray environment, described by the narrator as producing antibodies that bind to antigens.
entity_library.Antibody: Purple, Y-shaped molecular structures embedded on the surface of the plasma cell and later shown binding to a yellow particle, explained by the narrator as binding to toxins, bacteria, or antigens to render them harmless and mark them for macrophage recognition and phagocytosis.
entity_library.Opsonization: A process visually depicted by a pink antibody binding to a yellow particle in a red, blood-like environment, accompanied by on-screen text defining it. The narrator explicitly names this process as 'opsonization' and explains its purpose: marking antigens for easier recognition and quicker phagocytosis by macrophages.
entity_library.Human Immune Cells diagram: A dark, digital-style flowchart showing the differentiation of 'Stem Cell' into various immune cells, which the narrator refers to as illustrating how 'all immune cells have a stem cell as their origin' and develop into the 'immune cells just shown'.
entity_library.Megakaryocyte: A teal-colored, textured spherical cell attached to a red, ridged Blood vessel, extending pseudopodia to produce platelets. The narrator identifies it as the origin of platelets in the bone marrow and states that one megakaryocyte can produce 1,000 to 3,000 platelets.
entity_library.Opsonization text overlay: White text appearing in the lower right corner defining 'Opsonization' as 'Marking of specific particles by antibodies, especially to enhance phagocytosis,' which is explicitly named and explained by the narrator.
entity_library.Human Immune Cells title text: White text at the top of the dark diagram reading 'Human Immune Cells,' serving as the heading for the immune cell differentiation flowchart, which the narrator refers to when discussing the origin and development of 'the immune cells just shown'.
entity_library.Megakaryocyte label: White text identifying the teal cell as 'Megakaryocyte,' appearing next to the cell in the final frame, with the narrator providing functional context by mentioning 'platelets' as important immune cells.
entity_library.Blood vessel: A red, ridged tubular structure forming the inner lining of the vessel, shown in close-up with a bumpy, cellular surface, serving as the site of platelet release. The narrator mentions pseudopodia extending into these vessels in the bone marrow and later describes injury to a blood vessel initiating hemostasis.
entity_library.Pseudopodium: Long, branching, teal-colored extensions from the Megakaryocyte that extend into the blood vessel and fragment into platelets. The narrator explains that platelets are emitted into circulation through these pseudopodia.
entity_library.Thrombopoiesis: Text overlay defining the production of platelets (thrombocytes) by megakaryocytes in the bone marrow, appearing as the pseudopodia fragment. The narrator explicitly names and defines this process.
entity_library.Hemostasis: Text overlay describing all physiological processes that prevent and stop bleeding in case of blood vessel injuries, appearing as platelets flow through the vessel. The narrator introduces this process as beginning upon injury to a blood vessel.
entity_library.Platelets (thrombocytes): Small, disc-shaped, red entities flowing through the white network and accumulating, described by a male voice as forming a 'quite fragile platelet plug' initially, and later binding with 'fibrin strands' and 'red blood cells' to create a 'stronger plug' and ultimately 'a strong clot'.
entity_library.Fibrin network: A white, web-like lattice composed of interconnected spherical particles and thin filaments, forming a dense matrix that traps platelets, initially contributing to a 'fragile platelet plug', and later described by a male voice as 'fibrin strands' that form on the platelets to ensure a stronger, more stable clot.
